roadmap.title: 45-Day GenAI Engineer Roadmap
roadmap.description: A structured 45-day beginner curriculum designed to prepare students for entry-level Generative AI Engineer roles, covering Python, ML foundations, deep learning, NLP, transformers, LLMs, RAG, agents, and deployment.
roadmap.outcome: Ability to build, fine-tune, and deploy Generative AI applications using modern LLM tooling and MLOps practices.

days.title: Python Fundamentals for AI
objectives: Understand Python syntax, variables, and data types
Write conditional logic, loops, and functions
Set up a Python development environment for AI work
days.beginner_explanation: Python is the primary language of AI and machine learning because of its simple syntax and rich ecosystem of libraries. Today you learn the core building blocks: how to store data in variables, make decisions with if-statements, repeat work with loops, and package reusable logic into functions. You also set up a clean, isolated environment so your AI projects never conflict with each other.
days.practical_task: Install Python 3.11+, create a virtual environment, and write a script that takes a number and prints whether it is prime, using a loop and a function.
days.mini_project: Build a command-line 'AI budget calculator' that asks for monthly income and expenses, then prints savings, a warning if overspending, and a category breakdown using functions and dictionaries.
days.assignment: Write five small programs: a temperature converter, a FizzBuzz solution, a factorial calculator, a palindrome checker, and a simple calculator. Push them to a GitHub repository.

quiz.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
What does a virtual environment provide?
Which data type stores key-value pairs?

youtube.title: Python for Beginners - Learn Python in 1 Hour

research_papers.title: Python for Scientific Computing (Oliphant, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can write basic Python programs with loops, conditionals, and functions, and manage isolated project environments.

days.title: Python Data Structures and OOP
objectives: Master lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries
days.beginner_explanation: Data structures are how you organize information in memory. Choosing the right one (list vs dict vs set) affects performance and clarity. Object-oriented programming lets you model real-world things as objects with data (attributes) and behavior (methods), which is how most AI libraries are structured internally.
days.practical_task: Create a 'Dataset' class that stores a list of records, with methods to add, filter, and summarize records. Handle errors when a record is missing a field.
days.mini_project: Build a mini contact book with a Contact class and a ContactBook class supporting add, search, delete, and save to a JSON file.
days.assignment: Refactor Day 1's budget calculator into an OOP design with a Budget class, using exception handling for invalid input.

quiz.question: What does 'self' refer to in a class method?
Which keyword is used to handle exceptions?

research_papers.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (overview)

days.expected_outcome: You can model problems with classes and pick appropriate data structures for AI workflows.

days.title: NumPy for Numerical Computing
prerequisites: Day 1-2: Python basics and data structures
days.beginner_explanation: NumPy is the foundation of the entire scientific Python stack. It provides fast, memory-efficient arrays and vectorized operations that run in optimized C under the hood. Neural network tensors, embeddings, and matrices are all conceptually NumPy arrays, so understanding it is essential before deep learning.
days.practical_task: Create a 5x5 matrix of random numbers, compute row/column means, normalize the matrix, and find the max value's position.
days.mini_project: Implement a grayscale image manipulator: load an image as a NumPy array, then brighten, invert, and crop it using array operations.
days.assignment: Reimplement a dot product, matrix multiplication, and mean-squared-error calculation using only NumPy (no loops).

quiz.question: What does broadcasting allow?
options: Operations on arrays of different shapes
quiz.answer: Operations on arrays of different shapes

quiz.question: Which function creates an array of zeros?
What does axis=0 refer to in a 2D array?

research_papers.title: Array programming with NumPy (Harris et al., Nature 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can manipulate numerical data efficiently with NumPy arrays, a prerequisite for tensors.

days.title: Pandas for Data Handling
days.beginner_explanation: Pandas is the go-to tool for loading, cleaning, and exploring tabular data. Before you train any model, you must understand and prepare your data. Pandas DataFrames give you a spreadsheet-like interface backed by NumPy speed, and data preparation typically consumes most of a real ML project's time.
days.practical_task: Load a public CSV (e.g., Titanic), inspect it, handle missing values, and compute survival rate grouped by class and gender.
days.mini_project: Build a data-cleaning pipeline that ingests a messy CSV, standardizes column names, fills missing values, removes duplicates, and outputs a clean dataset with a summary report.
days.assignment: Analyze a dataset of your choice: produce 5 insights using groupby, filtering, and merging, documented in a Jupyter notebook.

quiz.question: Which method shows the first rows of a DataFrame?
How do you handle missing values?
What does groupby do?
options: Splits data into groups for aggregation
quiz.answer: Splits data into groups for aggregation

research_papers.title: Data Structures for Statistical Computing in Python (McKinney, 2010)

days.expected_outcome: You can load, clean, and analyze real-world tabular datasets confidently.

days.title: Data Visualization
days.beginner_explanation: Visualization turns raw numbers into understanding. As a GenAI engineer you will inspect data distributions, training curves, and model outputs constantly. Matplotlib gives fine-grained control while Seaborn produces beautiful statistical charts with minimal code.
days.practical_task: Using a dataset, create a histogram, a scatter plot with a trend, and a correlation heatmap, then interpret each.
days.mini_project: Build an exploratory data analysis (EDA) dashboard notebook for a dataset with at least 6 visualizations and written insights.
days.assignment: Recreate three chart types from a published data-journalism article and explain what makes them effective.

quiz.question: Which library is built on top of Matplotlib for statistics?
Which plot shows the distribution of a single variable?
What does a correlation heatmap show?

research_papers.title: Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment (Hunter, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can visualize data and communicate insights clearly through charts.

days.title: Linear Algebra for ML
objectives: Grasp eigenvalues and dot products intuitively
days.beginner_explanation: Linear algebra is the mathematical language of machine learning. Data is represented as vectors, models as matrices, and learning as transformations of these objects. Understanding dot products and matrix multiplication intuitively will make neural networks and embeddings far less mysterious.
days.practical_task: Using NumPy, compute the dot product, matrix product, transpose, and inverse of small matrices, and verify the inverse by multiplication.
days.mini_project: Implement a 2D linear transformation visualizer that shows how a matrix rotates, scales, and shears a set of points.
days.assignment: Work through 15 linear algebra problems (vector operations, matrix multiplication, eigen-decomposition) and verify each with NumPy.

quiz.question: What is the dot product of two orthogonal vectors?
Eigenvectors describe what property of a transformation?

research_papers.title: The Matrix Cookbook (Petersen & Pedersen)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the linear algebra that underpins neural networks and embeddings.

days.title: Calculus and Probability for ML
objectives: Grasp the chain rule for backpropagation
days.beginner_explanation: Calculus explains how models learn: gradients tell us which direction to adjust parameters to reduce error, and the chain rule makes backpropagation possible. Probability lets us reason about uncertainty, which is central to generative models that predict likely next tokens or pixels.
days.practical_task: Compute the gradient of a simple function f(x,y) by hand and verify numerically; simulate a coin-flip experiment to estimate probability.
days.mini_project: Implement gradient descent from scratch to minimize a quadratic function and plot the descent path.
days.assignment: Solve 10 derivative problems and 10 probability problems, including two Bayes' theorem applications.

quiz.question: What does a gradient point toward?

research_papers.title: Mathematics for Machine Learning (Deisenroth et al.)

days.expected_outcome: You understand gradients and probability well enough to reason about model training.

days.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
objectives: Understand what ML is and its types
Distinguish supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning
days.beginner_explanation: Machine learning is teaching computers to find patterns in data instead of hard-coding rules. Today you learn the big picture: the three main paradigms, how data is split for honest evaluation, and the central challenge of building models that generalize to new data rather than memorizing training examples.
days.practical_task: Split a dataset into train/validation/test sets and explain why each split matters using a simple example.
days.mini_project: Create an illustrated notebook that demonstrates overfitting vs underfitting by fitting polynomials of increasing degree to noisy data.
days.assignment: Write a one-page explainer classifying 10 real-world problems as supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning, with justification.

quiz.question: Which learning type uses labeled data?

youtube.title: Machine Learning for Everybody - Full Course

research_papers.title: A Few Useful Things to Know About Machine Learning (Domingos, 2012)

days.expected_outcome: You understand ML paradigms and the workflow used to build reliable models.

days.title: Supervised Learning Algorithms
days.beginner_explanation: Supervised learning maps inputs to known outputs. Regression predicts numbers (like house prices) while classification predicts categories (like spam vs not spam). Today you meet the foundational algorithms and see how a model 'learns' by minimizing a loss function via gradient descent.
days.practical_task: Train a linear regression model on a simple dataset and interpret its coefficients and error metrics.
days.mini_project: Build a house-price predictor with linear regression and a spam classifier with logistic regression, comparing their metrics.
days.assignment: Implement linear regression from scratch using NumPy and gradient descent, then compare against scikit-learn.

quiz.question: What does a loss function measure?

research_papers.title: Random Forests (Breiman, 2001)

days.expected_outcome: You can train and interpret basic supervised learning models.

days.title: Unsupervised Learning
days.beginner_explanation: Unsupervised learning finds structure in data without labels. Clustering groups similar items, and dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data (like embeddings) into fewer dimensions for visualization. These techniques are vital for exploring the embedding spaces used in modern GenAI.
days.practical_task: Apply K-Means to a dataset, choose k with the elbow method, and visualize clusters with PCA.
days.mini_project: Build a customer-segmentation tool that clusters users and visualizes segments in 2D using PCA.
days.assignment: Compare PCA and t-SNE on a high-dimensional dataset and discuss when each is appropriate.

research_papers.title: Visualizing Data using t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008)

days.expected_outcome: You can cluster and reduce the dimensionality of data, useful for embedding analysis.

days.title: Scikit-learn End-to-End
days.beginner_explanation: Scikit-learn is the standard toolkit for classical ML. Its consistent API (fit/predict) and pipelines let you chain preprocessing and modeling into a single reproducible object. Learning proper cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning here builds discipline you will carry into deep learning.
days.practical_task: Build a scikit-learn Pipeline that scales features, encodes categoricals, and trains a classifier, evaluated with cross-validation.
days.mini_project: Create a complete ML project on a Kaggle-style dataset: EDA, pipeline, tuning, evaluation, and saved model.
days.assignment: Use GridSearchCV to tune a model and report how tuning changed performance, with a confusion matrix.

research_papers.title: Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python (Pedregosa et al., 2011)

days.expected_outcome: You can build reproducible, tuned ML pipelines end-to-end.

days.title: Model Evaluation and Metrics
days.beginner_explanation: A model is only as good as how you measure it. Accuracy can be misleading on imbalanced data, so you must understand precision, recall, and F1 for classification, and MSE/MAE/R2 for regression. Correct metric selection is a core skill interviewers probe for.
days.practical_task: Compute precision, recall, F1, and plot an ROC curve for a classifier on imbalanced data.
days.mini_project: Build an evaluation report generator that, given predictions, outputs all key metrics, a confusion matrix, and an ROC plot.
days.assignment: Given a fraud-detection scenario, argue which metric matters most and defend your choice in writing.

quiz.question: Which metric balances precision and recall?

research_papers.title: The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves (Davis & Goadrich, 2006)

days.expected_outcome: You can choose and interpret the right metrics for any ML task.

days.title: Introduction to Neural Networks
days.beginner_explanation: Neural networks stack simple units (neurons) into layers that can learn complex patterns. Each neuron computes a weighted sum, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear activation. Stacking these lets the network approximate almost any function, which is why they power modern generative AI.
days.practical_task: Draw and compute by hand a forward pass through a 2-layer network with ReLU activations for a small input.
days.mini_project: Implement a simple feedforward neural network from scratch in NumPy that classifies XOR.
days.assignment: Explain, with diagrams, how activation functions introduce non-linearity and why that matters.

quiz.question: What introduces non-linearity in a network?

youtube.title: But what is a neural network?

research_papers.title: Multilayer Feedforward Networks are Universal Approximators (Hornik, 1989)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how neural networks compute predictions.

days.title: Deep Learning Fundamentals
days.beginner_explanation: Deep learning is training multi-layer networks using backpropagation, which applies the chain rule to compute how each weight affects the loss. Optimizers like Adam use these gradients to update weights efficiently, while techniques like dropout prevent overfitting on large models.
days.practical_task: Trace how backpropagation updates a single weight in a tiny network, computing the gradient step by step.
days.mini_project: Extend your NumPy network from Day 13 to include backpropagation and train it on a small dataset.
days.assignment: Experiment with three learning rates and two optimizers, then plot and compare training curves.

youtube.title: The spelled-out intro to neural networks and backpropagation (micrograd)

research_papers.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization (Kingma & Ba, 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how deep networks are trained and regularized.

days.title: PyTorch Basics
objectives: Build and train a model in PyTorch
days.beginner_explanation: PyTorch is the dominant framework for GenAI research and production. Its tensors behave like NumPy arrays but run on GPUs and track gradients automatically via autograd. Mastering the standard training loop here is essential for everything that follows with transformers and LLMs.
days.practical_task: Build a PyTorch model with nn.Module to classify handwritten digits (MNIST) and write the training loop manually.
days.mini_project: Train an MNIST classifier in PyTorch, log accuracy per epoch, and save/load the trained model.
days.assignment: Reimplement your Day 14 network in PyTorch and compare training convenience and results.

youtube.title: PyTorch for Deep Learning - Full Course

research_papers.title: PyTorch: An Imperative Style, High-Performance Deep Learning Library (Paszke et al., 2019)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and train neural networks in PyTorch.

days.title: TensorFlow and Keras Basics
objectives: Understand the Sequential and Functional APIs
days.beginner_explanation: TensorFlow with Keras offers a high-level, beginner-friendly way to build neural networks with minimal code. While PyTorch dominates research, many production systems and tutorials use Keras, so knowing both frameworks makes you versatile and employable.
days.practical_task: Build and train an image classifier using the Keras Sequential API on the Fashion-MNIST dataset.
days.mini_project: Create the same model in both Keras and PyTorch and write a short comparison of the developer experience.
days.assignment: Use Keras callbacks (early stopping, checkpointing) and document how they change training behavior.

research_papers.title: TensorFlow: A System for Large-Scale Machine Learning (Abadi et al., 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and train models with Keras and understand both major frameworks.

days.title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
days.beginner_explanation: CNNs are specialized networks for images. Convolution layers detect local patterns (edges, textures) that combine into complex features. Understanding CNNs matters for GenAI because image-generation models like diffusion models build on convolutional foundations.
days.practical_task: Build a CNN in PyTorch or Keras to classify CIFAR-10 images and report accuracy.
days.mini_project: Use transfer learning with a pretrained ResNet to classify a custom set of images with high accuracy.
days.assignment: Compare a from-scratch CNN vs a transfer-learning model on the same dataset and discuss the difference.

research_papers.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep CNNs (AlexNet, Krizhevsky et al., 2012)

days.expected_outcome: You can build image classifiers and apply transfer learning.

days.title: RNNs, LSTMs and Sequence Models
days.beginner_explanation: Before transformers, RNNs and LSTMs were the standard for text and sequences. They process data step by step, keeping a memory of previous inputs. Understanding their limitations, especially difficulty with long-range dependencies, explains exactly why the transformer was invented.
days.practical_task: Build an LSTM that predicts the next character in a text sequence and generate a short sample.
days.mini_project: Train a character-level LSTM text generator on a small corpus (e.g., song lyrics) and sample new text.
days.assignment: Write an explainer comparing RNN, LSTM, and GRU, highlighting the vanishing gradient problem.

quiz.question: A key limitation of RNNs is...

research_papers.title: Long Short-Term Memory (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997)

days.expected_outcome: You understand sequence models and why transformers replaced them.

days.title: Introduction to NLP
days.beginner_explanation: Natural Language Processing is teaching computers to work with human language, the core of GenAI. Before deep models, text was turned into numbers using techniques like TF-IDF. Learning these classical methods builds intuition for how machines represent meaning in text.
days.practical_task: Preprocess a set of documents (tokenize, remove stopwords, lemmatize) and build a TF-IDF sentiment classifier.
days.mini_project: Build a movie-review sentiment analyzer using TF-IDF and logistic regression, reporting accuracy and top features.
days.assignment: Compare bag-of-words vs TF-IDF on a classification task and explain the difference in results.

research_papers.title: A Statistical Interpretation of Term Specificity (TF-IDF, Sparck Jones, 1972)

days.expected_outcome: You can preprocess text and build classical NLP classifiers.

days.title: Word Embeddings
days.beginner_explanation: Word embeddings represent words as dense vectors where similar words are close together. The famous example 'king - man + woman = queen' shows meaning encoded as geometry. Embeddings are the foundation of modern NLP and the retrieval systems used in RAG.
days.practical_task: Load pretrained GloVe embeddings, find nearest neighbors of a word, and test vector arithmetic analogies.
days.mini_project: Build a word-similarity explorer that visualizes embeddings in 2D and answers analogy queries.
days.assignment: Train a small Word2Vec model on a corpus and analyze the clusters that emerge.

quiz.question: Skip-gram predicts...
options: Context words from a center word
quiz.answer: Context words from a center word

quiz.question: A limitation of static embeddings is...

youtube.title: Word Embedding and Word2Vec, Clearly Explained

research_papers.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space (Word2Vec, Mikolov et al., 2013)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how words become vectors, foundational for embeddings and RAG.

days.title: The Attention Mechanism
days.beginner_explanation: Attention lets a model focus on the most relevant parts of the input when producing each output. Instead of reading sequentially like an RNN, self-attention compares every token to every other token in parallel. This single idea unlocked the transformer and the entire modern LLM era.
days.practical_task: Compute scaled dot-product attention by hand for a tiny 3-token example and interpret the weights.
days.mini_project: Implement single-head self-attention in NumPy/PyTorch and visualize the attention matrix for a sentence.
days.assignment: Explain, with a worked example, how query-key-value produces attention weights and why scaling is used.

youtube.title: Attention for Neural Networks, Clearly Explained

research_papers.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate (Bahdanau et al., 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the attention mechanism that powers transformers.

days.title: The Transformer Architecture
days.beginner_explanation: The transformer combines self-attention, positional encodings, and feedforward layers into a highly parallelizable architecture. Because it processes all tokens at once and scales well with data and compute, it became the backbone of GPT, BERT, and every modern LLM.
days.practical_task: Read 'The Illustrated Transformer' and annotate a diagram labeling each component and its purpose.
days.mini_project: Follow Karpathy's nanoGPT lecture to build a tiny GPT that generates text character by character.
days.assignment: Write a technical summary of the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper in your own words.

youtube.title: Let's build GPT: from scratch, in code, spelled out
Transformers, explained: Understand the model behind GPT

research_papers.title: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the transformer architecture end-to-end and can build a tiny GPT.

days.title: BERT and Encoder Models
days.beginner_explanation: BERT is an encoder-only transformer trained to fill in masked words, giving it deep bidirectional understanding of text. It excels at understanding tasks like classification, named-entity recognition, and question answering, and its contextual embeddings power many production search systems.
days.practical_task: Use a pretrained BERT model to generate contextual embeddings and compare word meanings in different sentences.
days.mini_project: Fine-tune DistilBERT for sentiment classification on a small dataset using Hugging Face.
days.assignment: Explain masked language modeling and how BERT's bidirectionality differs from GPT's left-to-right approach.

quiz.question: BERT is which type of model?

research_papers.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers (Devlin et al., 2018)

days.expected_outcome: You understand and can fine-tune encoder models like BERT.

days.title: GPT and Decoder Models
days.beginner_explanation: GPT models are decoder-only transformers trained to predict the next token, generating text one token at a time. This simple objective, scaled to massive data and parameters, produces the fluent, capable models that define generative AI today. Understanding autoregression is key to using LLMs well.
days.practical_task: Use a small GPT-2 model to generate text and experiment with how prompts affect output.
days.mini_project: Extend your nanoGPT from Day 22 to train on a new text corpus and generate themed text.
days.assignment: Explain how next-token prediction leads to emergent capabilities, referencing scaling laws.

research_papers.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3, Brown et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how GPT-style models generate text.

days.title: Hugging Face Transformers
days.beginner_explanation: Hugging Face is the GitHub of machine learning models. Its Transformers library lets you download and run thousands of pretrained models in a few lines of code. This is the single most important practical tool for a working GenAI engineer.
days.practical_task: Use the pipeline API for sentiment analysis, summarization, and text generation with different models.
days.mini_project: Build a multi-task NLP web app (sentiment, summarization, Q&A) using Hugging Face pipelines and Streamlit.
days.assignment: Explore the Hub, pick three models for one task, and benchmark their speed and quality.

quiz.question: The pipeline API is used to...
AutoTokenizer does what?
options: Loads the correct tokenizer for a model
quiz.answer: Loads the correct tokenizer for a model

research_papers.title: Transformers: State-of-the-Art Natural Language Processing (Wolf et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can load and run any pretrained model from Hugging Face.

days.title: Tokenization Deep Dive
objectives: Handle special tokens and context limits
days.beginner_explanation: Tokenization is how text becomes numbers a model can process. Modern models use subword tokenization (like BPE) that balances vocabulary size and flexibility. Understanding tokens is practical: it affects context limits, API costs, and why models sometimes struggle with spelling or math.
days.practical_task: Tokenize sentences with several tokenizers, count tokens, and observe how BPE splits rare words.
days.mini_project: Build a token-counter and cost-estimator tool for LLM API calls across different models.
days.assignment: Explain BPE with a worked example of merging character pairs into subwords.

quiz.question: BPE tokenization works at the level of...
The context window limits...
options: How many tokens a model can process
quiz.answer: How many tokens a model can process

research_papers.title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units (BPE, Sennrich et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You understand tokenization and its practical impact on LLM usage.

days.title: Pretraining vs Fine-tuning
objectives: Learn instruction tuning and RLHF basics
days.beginner_explanation: Modern LLMs are built in stages: expensive pretraining on huge text corpora gives general knowledge, then fine-tuning adapts the model to specific tasks or behaviors. Instruction tuning and RLHF make models helpful and safe. Knowing this pipeline helps you decide when to fine-tune versus simply prompt a model.
days.practical_task: Create a decision flowchart for when to use prompting, fine-tuning, or RAG for a given business problem.
days.mini_project: Write a comparative report on three real models (base, instruction-tuned, RLHF) and their intended uses.
days.assignment: Summarize how RLHF turns a base model into a helpful assistant, in under 500 words.

youtube.title: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) Explained

research_papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT, Ouyang et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can decide between prompting, fine-tuning, and RAG for a use case.

days.title: Fine-tuning LLMs
days.beginner_explanation: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained model to your specific data or style. You format examples, run supervised training with the Hugging Face Trainer, and evaluate the results. Even small fine-tunes can dramatically improve performance on niche tasks, a highly marketable GenAI skill.
days.practical_task: Fine-tune DistilGPT-2 on a small custom text dataset and generate samples before and after.
days.mini_project: Fine-tune a small model to answer questions in a specific tone or domain and demonstrate the improvement.
days.assignment: Document a full fine-tuning experiment including dataset prep, config, metrics, and lessons learned.

research_papers.title: Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification (ULMFiT, Howard & Ruder, 2018)

days.expected_outcome: You can fine-tune a small language model on custom data.

days.title: LoRA and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
days.beginner_explanation: Full fine-tuning of large models needs enormous GPU memory. LoRA solves this by freezing the base model and training tiny 'adapter' matrices, cutting trainable parameters by over 99%. QLoRA adds quantization so you can fine-tune billion-parameter models on a single consumer GPU, a game-changing practical skill.
days.practical_task: Fine-tune a 7B model with LoRA using the PEFT library on a small instruction dataset in a free GPU notebook.
days.mini_project: Create a domain-specific assistant by fine-tuning an open model with QLoRA and publish the adapter to the Hub.
days.assignment: Compare full fine-tuning vs LoRA in terms of memory, speed, and quality, with a written analysis.

youtube.title: LoRA explained (and a bit about precision and quantization)

research_papers.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (Hu et al., 2021)
QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs (Dettmers et al., 2023)

days.expected_outcome: You can efficiently fine-tune large models on limited hardware using LoRA/QLoRA.

days.title: Prompt Engineering
days.beginner_explanation: Prompt engineering is the art of communicating with LLMs to get reliable results. Techniques like few-shot examples and chain-of-thought reasoning dramatically improve output quality without any training. This is the fastest, cheapest way to control model behavior and a daily skill for GenAI engineers.
days.practical_task: Take one task and iteratively improve results using zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompts.
days.mini_project: Build a prompt library with reusable templates for summarization, extraction, classification, and reasoning tasks.
days.assignment: Document a prompt-optimization case study showing before/after outputs and what changed.

quiz.question: Few-shot prompting provides...
options: A few examples in the prompt
quiz.answer: A few examples in the prompt

research_papers.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in LLMs (Wei et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can reliably steer LLM behavior through effective prompting.

days.title: LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Open Models)
days.beginner_explanation: Most GenAI products are built on hosted LLM APIs. You learn to authenticate securely, structure chat messages, control randomness with temperature, stream tokens for responsive UIs, and request structured JSON output. These are the concrete integration skills interviewers expect.
days.practical_task: Write a Python script that calls an LLM API with a system prompt, streams the response, and returns structured JSON.
days.mini_project: Build a CLI chatbot with conversation memory using an LLM API, with streaming and token-cost tracking.
days.assignment: Implement robust API error handling with retries and rate-limit backoff, and document your approach.

research_papers.title: GPT-4 Technical Report (OpenAI, 2023)

days.expected_outcome: You can integrate commercial LLM APIs into applications securely.

days.title: LangChain Basics
days.beginner_explanation: LangChain is a framework for building LLM applications by composing reusable components: models, prompts, parsers, memory, and tools. It standardizes common patterns so you can build chatbots, RAG systems, and agents faster. It is one of the most in-demand GenAI frameworks.
days.practical_task: Build a LangChain chain that takes a topic, generates an outline, then expands each section, using LCEL.
days.mini_project: Create a conversational assistant with LangChain that remembers context across turns and uses a custom prompt template.
days.assignment: Rebuild your Day 31 chatbot in LangChain and compare the two approaches in a short write-up.

research_papers.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (Yao et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can build LLM applications by composing LangChain components.

days.title: Embeddings and Semantic Search
days.beginner_explanation: Embeddings turn text into vectors that capture meaning, so semantically similar texts have similar vectors. Semantic search finds relevant documents by vector similarity rather than exact keywords. This is the retrieval engine behind RAG, the most common GenAI architecture in production.
days.practical_task: Embed a set of documents, then given a query, retrieve the top-k most similar using cosine similarity.
days.mini_project: Build a semantic search engine over a collection of articles with adjustable chunking and top-k retrieval.
days.assignment: Experiment with three chunk sizes and measure their effect on retrieval quality for sample queries.

quiz.question: Chunking is needed because...
options: Documents exceed context and retrieval granularity matters
quiz.answer: Documents exceed context and retrieval granularity matters

research_papers.title: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019)

days.expected_outcome: You can build semantic search systems using embeddings.

days.title: Vector Databases
objectives: Store and query embeddings at scale
days.beginner_explanation: Vector databases store millions of embeddings and retrieve the most similar ones in milliseconds using approximate nearest neighbor indexes. They add persistence, metadata filtering, and scalability to semantic search. Every production RAG system relies on a vector database.
days.practical_task: Load documents into Chroma or FAISS, run similarity queries, and filter results using metadata.
days.mini_project: Migrate your Day 33 search engine to a vector database with persistence and metadata filters.
days.assignment: Compare two vector databases (e.g., Chroma vs FAISS) on setup, speed, and features in a written report.

quiz.question: Vector databases use which index type?

research_papers.title: Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW (Malkov & Yashunin, 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can store and query embeddings at scale with a vector database.

days.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Fundamentals
days.beginner_explanation: RAG lets an LLM answer questions using your private, up-to-date data by retrieving relevant chunks and injecting them into the prompt. This grounds responses in facts, reduces hallucinations, and avoids expensive retraining. RAG is the single most common GenAI architecture in industry.
days.practical_task: Build a minimal RAG pipeline: embed docs, retrieve top-k for a query, and generate a grounded answer with citations.
days.mini_project: Build a 'chat with your PDF' app that answers questions from an uploaded document using RAG.
days.assignment: Demonstrate, with examples, how RAG reduces hallucinations compared to a plain LLM on domain questions.

quiz.question: RAG is preferred over fine-tuning when...
options: Knowledge is dynamic and factual grounding is needed
quiz.answer: Knowledge is dynamic and factual grounding is needed

research_papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks (Lewis et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You understand and can build a basic RAG pipeline.

days.title: Building a Production RAG Pipeline
days.beginner_explanation: A production RAG system needs more than basic retrieval: careful chunking, hybrid keyword+vector search, reranking to surface the best chunks, and evaluation to measure quality. Today you assemble these into a robust pipeline and learn to measure whether your RAG actually works.
days.practical_task: Add a reranker to your Day 35 RAG app and measure the improvement in answer relevance.
days.mini_project: Build a documentation Q&A assistant with hybrid search, reranking, citations, and a RAGAS evaluation report.
days.assignment: Evaluate your RAG system with RAGAS metrics and write recommendations to improve weak areas.

research_papers.title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain QA (Karpukhin et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and evaluate a production-quality RAG system.

days.title: LLM Agents and Tool Use
days.beginner_explanation: Agents let LLMs take actions, not just generate text. Through tool calling, a model can search the web, run code, query databases, or call APIs, then reason over the results to solve multi-step problems. Agentic systems are the current frontier of applied GenAI.
days.practical_task: Build an agent that can use a calculator tool and a search tool to answer multi-step questions.
days.mini_project: Create a research assistant agent that searches, retrieves, and synthesizes information into a cited summary.
days.assignment: Design and implement three custom tools for an agent and document how the agent decides when to use each.

research_papers.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools (Schick et al., 2023)

days.expected_outcome: You can build LLM agents that use tools to solve multi-step tasks.

days.title: Diffusion Models and Image Generation
days.beginner_explanation: Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse a process that gradually adds noise, starting from random noise and denoising into a coherent image guided by a text prompt. They power tools like Stable Diffusion and are essential for the visual side of generative AI.
days.practical_task: Use the Diffusers library to generate images from text prompts and experiment with guidance scale and steps.
days.mini_project: Build a text-to-image web app with prompt controls and image-to-image editing using Stable Diffusion.
days.assignment: Explain the forward and reverse diffusion processes and how text conditioning guides generation.

quiz.question: Guidance scale controls...
options: How closely output follows the prompt
quiz.answer: How closely output follows the prompt

research_papers.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (Ho et al., 2020)
High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models (Rombach et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can generate and edit images with diffusion models.

days.title: Multimodal Models
days.beginner_explanation: Multimodal models understand more than one type of data at once, such as images and text together. CLIP aligns images and text in a shared space, and vision-language models can describe images or answer questions about them. Whisper transcribes speech. These unlock rich, real-world GenAI applications.
days.practical_task: Use CLIP to match images to text captions and a vision-language model to answer questions about an image.
days.mini_project: Build a multimodal assistant that accepts an image and a question and returns a grounded answer, plus Whisper voice input.
days.assignment: Explain how CLIP's contrastive training aligns images and text, with a diagram.

quiz.question: CLIP aligns...
options: Images and text in a shared space
quiz.answer: Images and text in a shared space

research_papers.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP, Radford et al., 2021)

days.expected_outcome: You can build applications combining images, text, and audio.

days.title: LLM Evaluation and Guardrails
days.beginner_explanation: Shipping GenAI safely requires measuring quality and preventing harm. You learn evaluation methods including LLM-as-judge, plus guardrails that block unsafe content and defend against prompt injection. Employers increasingly demand these responsible-AI skills before deployment.
days.practical_task: Set up an LLM-as-judge evaluation for a set of model outputs and add an input/output moderation guardrail.
days.mini_project: Build an evaluation harness that scores your RAG app's answers and flags hallucinations and unsafe content.
days.assignment: Design a test suite of adversarial prompts (including injection attempts) and report how your guardrails respond.

research_papers.title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena (Zheng et al., 2023)

days.expected_outcome: You can evaluate and safeguard LLM applications for production.

days.title: Deploying GenAI Applications
objectives: Build an API around an LLM app
days.beginner_explanation: A model is only useful when others can access it. You wrap your LLM logic in a FastAPI backend, build a UI with Streamlit or Gradio, containerize with Docker, and deploy to the cloud. These full-stack deployment skills turn prototypes into real products employers value.
days.practical_task: Wrap your RAG app in a FastAPI endpoint with streaming and connect a simple Gradio front end.
days.mini_project: Deploy your RAG chatbot as a containerized web app to a free cloud host with a public URL.
days.assignment: Write a deployment guide documenting your architecture, Dockerfile, and environment configuration.

quiz.question: Secrets like API keys should be...

youtube.title: Build and Deploy an AI App

research_papers.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can deploy a GenAI application as a live web service.

days.title: LLMOps and Monitoring
days.beginner_explanation: LLMOps applies DevOps discipline to LLM applications: monitoring quality and cost, tracing requests to debug failures, versioning prompts and models, and caching to save money. These operational skills separate hobby projects from production systems and are increasingly required for GenAI roles.
days.practical_task: Add tracing to your deployed app with LangSmith and implement response caching to reduce repeated API costs.
days.mini_project: Instrument your GenAI app with a monitoring dashboard tracking latency, cost, token usage, and user feedback.
days.assignment: Analyze one week of simulated logs and propose three optimizations for cost and latency.

research_papers.title: Challenges in Deploying Machine Learning: a Survey of Case Studies (Paleyes et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can monitor, trace, and optimize GenAI applications in production.

days.title: Capstone Project - Part 1: Design and Build
days.beginner_explanation: The capstone ties everything together. Today you choose a meaningful problem, design a clean architecture combining RAG and/or agents, and implement the core functionality with a proper project structure. A strong capstone is the centerpiece of a GenAI job application.
days.practical_task: Write a one-page project spec (problem, users, architecture diagram, tech stack) and scaffold the repository.
days.mini_project: Build the core of a full GenAI application, for example an AI knowledge assistant with RAG and tool-using agents.
days.assignment: Implement the ingestion pipeline and core generation logic with clean, documented, version-controlled code.

youtube.title: Build a Full Stack AI SaaS App

research_papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks (Lewis et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You have a working core of a real GenAI application.

days.title: Capstone Project - Part 2: Evaluate, Deploy, Polish
days.beginner_explanation: A great project is finished, safe, and presentable. Today you add evaluation and guardrails, deploy to a public URL, set up monitoring, and write clear documentation. Polishing the user experience and demo turns a good project into a portfolio piece that lands interviews.
days.practical_task: Add guardrails and an evaluation report to the capstone, then deploy it publicly with monitoring enabled.
days.mini_project: Finish and deploy the full capstone with a polished UI, live demo, and comprehensive README.
days.assignment: Record a 3-minute demo video and write a technical README covering architecture, setup, and results.

quiz.question: A portfolio project should include...
options: Clear README and a live demo
quiz.answer: Clear README and a live demo

youtube.title: How to Write a Great README

research_papers.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You have a deployed, documented, portfolio-ready GenAI capstone.

days.title: Portfolio, Interview Prep, and Career Launch
days.beginner_explanation: Skills get you interviews only if people can see them. Today you polish your GitHub, write a targeted resume, and rehearse common GenAI interview questions covering transformers, RAG, fine-tuning, and system design. You also set up habits to keep learning as the field evolves rapidly.
days.practical_task: Polish your GitHub profile and pin your best projects with clear READMEs, then draft a GenAI-focused resume.
days.mini_project: Create a personal portfolio site showcasing your capstone and key projects with descriptions and live links.
days.assignment: Answer 20 GenAI interview questions in writing and complete one mock LLM system-design question.

quiz.question: The most important portfolio asset is...
For a RAG system design question you should discuss...
options: Ingestion, retrieval, generation, eval, and scaling
quiz.answer: Ingestion, retrieval, generation, eval, and scaling

youtube.title: How to Build a Developer Portfolio

research_papers.title: A Survey of Large Language Models (Zhao et al., 2023)

days.expected_outcome: You are ready to apply and interview for entry-level GenAI Engineer roles.
